hero.title: Ljubljana
hero.sub:
Everything you need to know before you put your bag down.
Written for the person who just landed — in the rare European capital where the honest price and the tourist price are nearly the same number.

orient.title: Orient yourself in sixty seconds
orient.lede: Ljubljana is the smallest capital in this guide and the easiest to hold in your head. A green river loops around a castle hill; a car-free old town sits on one bank, the cafés and squares on the other. You can cross the entire centre on foot in twenty minutes, and you will.

map.caption: Not to scale. The castle sits on the hill above the Old Town. Nothing in the centre is more than a twenty-minute walk from anything else.

facts.def: The euro. No exchange booths preying on you, no koruna arithmetic, no dual pricing. Slovenia joined the eurozone in 2007 and it shows.
Slovenian. English is spoken at a level that will genuinely surprise you — near-universal under fifty. Try dober dan anyway; it lands.
Excellent and free. Ask for voda iz pipe. There are drinking fountains through the centre.
112 from any phone, any language. Police 113.

facts.term: You will not need a bus
facts.def: The Old Town is closed to cars and the whole centre is walkable. Read the transport section anyway, because the fare has a trick in it worth €0.20 and a €2 card.

Walk one bridge upstream. Ljubljana has no real tourist trap — what it has is a riverside terrace where everything costs about a euro more. That euro buys a genuinely lovely view. Just don't eat all three meals on it.

arrive.title: From the airport, without being robbed
arrive.lede: Ljubljana Jože Pučnik Airport is 26 km north of the city, which is further than it sounds and served less frequently than you'd like. This is the one part of the trip where planning ahead pays for itself several times over.

options.title: Public bus 28
options.time: ≈ 50 min to the main bus station
options.body: An ordinary public bus. Slow, infrequent, and by a wide margin the cheapest way into town. Bring small notes — the driver will not love a fifty.
options.note: This is the one price on this page we cannot pin down. Published figures range from €1.30 (weekend, older listings) to €4.10 (2026). Carry €5 and expect the higher end.

options.title: Shared shuttle
options.body: Nomago and GoOpti both run shared vans that drop you at your address. Booked early, this is the sweet spot: a fraction of a taxi, a fraction of the wait for a bus.
options.note: Book it before you fly. The same GoOpti seat sold last-minute climbs to €50 or more. This is dynamic pricing, not a scam, and it punishes the unprepared.

options.title: Bolt
options.body: Metered, in-app, no negotiation. If there are two or three of you, this beats the shuttle on both money and dignity.
options.note: Fine at any hour. The price you see is the price you pay.

options.title: The car with no meter
options.body: Unofficial drivers work the airport arrivals area and the Central Bus Station. No meter, no company logo, no receipt. An official taxi runs €35–50; an unofficial one asks more and offers less.
options.note: Never take a car without a working meter and a visible company name. Open Bolt instead — it takes forty seconds.

transit.title: Public transport, decoded
transit.lede: One bus network, one flat fare, ninety minutes of free transfers. And a genuine trick: the way you pay changes the price.

rows.label: Single ride, contactless bank card
values: Tap your own card on the validator

rows.label: The Urbana card itself
values: One-off. Holds up to €50 of credit

callouts.title: Don't buy the Urbana card
callouts.body: Tapping your own contactless bank card on the validator costs €1.30. Buying the tourist-facing Urbana card costs €2.00 up front and then charges you €1.50 a ride. Unless you are here a fortnight, the card you already own is cheaper.

callouts.title: You probably won't ride a bus at all
callouts.body: The Old Town is closed to traffic and the centre is twenty minutes end to end on foot. Most visitors use the bus exactly once: to and from the airport.

callouts.title: The airport fare is genuinely unclear
callouts.body: We checked several sources and they disagree — €1.30, €3.30, €4.10, depending on the year and the day of the week. We have printed the highest recent figure so you are not caught short. We would rather over-warn you than have you standing at a bus door with the wrong coins.

money.title: Money: there is almost nothing to warn you about
money.lede: This section is short, and that is the finding. Slovenia sits in the top ten of the Global Peace Index, uses the euro, and has no meaningful exchange racket, taxi-meter fraud, or menu scam. Three small things remain.

rules.title: Cards work almost everywhere. Bureks do not take them
rules.body: Restaurants, bars, shops and buses all take contactless without blinking. The exceptions are the ones you'll actually want: the burek windows, the market stalls on Vodnik Square, and the odd tiny bar. Keep €20 in cash and you'll never be embarrassed.

rules.title: When the terminal offers you your own currency, say no
rules.body: If your card isn't euro-denominated, the machine may offer to charge you in pounds or dollars. That is dynamic currency conversion, and the rate is invented by the merchant's terminal. Always choose EUR and let your own bank convert.

rules.title: Tipping is not expected — round up anyway
rules.body: Nobody in Ljubljana depends on your tip and nobody will chase you down the street. Rounding up, or 10% after a genuinely good meal, is welcomed and entirely optional. There is no service charge to check for.

money.foot: Ljubljana's principal threat to your wallet is a second slice of potica. We have written this section as honestly as we can and there simply isn't more to say.

converter.title: Quick sanity check
converter.lede: For visitors arriving from outside the eurozone.
converter.tip: Your yardstick is a beer and a burek. A large domestic lager is €3 in a pub and €4.20 by the river. A burek is €3 and will defeat you. If you're paying much more, you're sitting on the water — which is allowed.

sleep.title: Where to sleep
sleep.lede: We recommend neighbourhoods, not hotels. In Ljubljana that advice comes with a caveat: the city is so small that the neighbourhood barely moves the price. It moves the character, and how much sleep you get.

hoods.name: Old Town & Centre
hoods.verdict: Car-free, cobbled, and the loveliest place to wake up.
hoods.body: Between Prešeren Square and the castle hill. No traffic, a river running through it, and every sight you came for within eight minutes. The trade is summer nights: the riverside bars beneath your window run late and they run loud.

image.alt: A pedestrian street in central Ljubljana.
image.caption: Old Town & Centre — car-free, cobbled, loud in summer.

hoods.name: Trnovo
hoods.verdict: Fifteen minutes' walk and a different city.
hoods.body: The bohemian south bank: leafy streets, galleries, weekend markets, cafés with nobody's suitcase in them. Close enough to walk into the centre for dinner, far enough that you'll sleep afterwards.

image.alt: Miklošičeva street in Ljubljana.
image.caption: Trnovo — quiet and bohemian. Representative street.

hoods.name: Tabor
hoods.verdict: The cheapest beds, five minutes from the square.
hoods.body: Next to the train and bus stations, which sounds worse than it is — this is Ljubljana, and the station district is a residential neighbourhood with good cafés. If you are counting euros, count them here.

image.alt: A central Ljubljana street.
image.caption: Tabor — the cheapest beds, five minutes from the square.

hoods.name: Beside Metelkova
hoods.verdict: Wonderful to visit. Difficult to sleep next to.
hoods.body: Metelkova is a former army barracks turned autonomous art squat — murals, sculpture, live music, and a genuine counterculture that has survived thirty years. It is one of the best things in the city. It is also loud until four in the morning.

image.alt: The Metelkova art quarter, Ljubljana.
image.caption: Beside Metelkova — wonderful to visit, hard to sleep next to.

sleep.tipsTitle: Three things worth knowing before you book

tips.title: Book on quiet, not on distance.
tips.body: Everything is walkable, so proximity buys you nothing and costs you a night's sleep. Ask which way the room faces and whether there is a bar below.

tips.title: Citywide, the bands are narrow.
tips.body: Budget rooms sit around €29 a night, mid-range around €69, and the top of the market around €147. There is no €400 tier here, and that is part of the appeal.

tips.title: Slovenia charges a nightly tourist tax
tips.body: per adult, collected at the property and usually absent from your online total. It is a euro or two. It is not a scam.

band.alt: People crossing the Triple Bridge towards Prešeren Square in the evening.
band.caption: The whole centre is twenty minutes end to end on foot. Book on quiet, not on distance — you will not need a bus.

hotels.title: A few specific hotels
hotels.lede: Named hotels, not just districts — you asked. Same honesty rule as the restaurants: we have not slept in these. Prices are indicative and move with the season; guest scores are the aggregate on the big booking sites, not ours. Check current rates before you book.

items.name: Emonec Hotel
items.desc: A plain, central, riverside three-star — the honest budget pick, two minutes from Prešeren Square.

image.alt: A pedestrian street in central Ljubljana.
image.caption: The riverside old town nearby — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: City Hotel Ljubljana
items.desc: A big, reliable three-star a few minutes from the Old Town. Consistently high scores for the price.

image.alt: Miklošičeva street in Ljubljana.
image.caption: A central street by it — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: BW Premier Hotel Slon
items.desc: A four-star on the main street, Slovenska cesta, in the thick of it. Mid-upper, central.

image.alt: Slovenska cesta in Ljubljana.
image.caption: On Slovenska cesta — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: Grand Hotel Union
items.desc: The Art-Nouveau landmark on Miklošičeva, green dome and all. Historic and well-rated.

image.alt: The Art-Nouveau Grand Hotel Union building, Ljubljana.

items.name: Hostel Celica
items.desc: A former military prison in the Metelkova art quarter, each cell restyled by an artist. Cheap, unique, lively.

image.alt: A pedestrian street in central Ljubljana.
image.caption: Central Ljubljana nearby — representative, not the hostel.

items.name: B&B Hotel Ljubljana
items.desc: A no-surprises budget-mid chain hotel, walkable to the old town. What you book when you just want a clean room.

image.alt: A pedestrian street in central Ljubljana.
image.caption: A central street by it — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: Hotel Cubo
items.desc: A slick, muted design hotel on Slovenska cesta with a well-regarded restaurant. The stylish mid-upper pick.

image.alt: Miklošičeva street in Ljubljana.
image.caption: A central street by it — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: Vander Urbani Resort
items.desc: A design boutique folded into old town houses by the river, with a tiny rooftop pool. Central and quietly luxe.

image.alt: The Art-Nouveau Grand Hotel Union, Ljubljana.
image.caption: The old-town centre — representative, not the hotel.

eat.title: Eat and drink like you live here
eat.lede: In Prague the gap between the local price and the tourist price is a factor of two. In Vienna it is a beautiful room. In Ljubljana it is about one euro — and the euro buys you a river.

compare.title: The same thing, two prices
compare.lede: Left: a normal pub or café, one street back. Right: a terrace on the Ljubljanica.

rows.badNote: a bela kava on the embankment

rows.itemNote: burek, from a window, standing up
rows.badNote: three courses in the Old Town

compare.foot: One euro. That is the entire tourist premium in this city, and the view is worth more than that. We have spent this section telling you what things cost rather than warning you what to avoid, because in Ljubljana there is very little to avoid.

places.title: Four places to actually eat
places.lede: Why we name restaurants but not hotels: a bad meal costs you €10 and an evening, a bad hotel costs you €500 and a week. And a burek window that has been open since 1972 is a verifiable fact in a way that "lovely rooms" is not. Every address below is checked against an official listing. We have still not eaten in any of them.

items.name: Klobasarna
items.body: Six seats and a counter, serving the one thing it makes: kranjska klobasa, the Carniolan sausage whose recipe is protected by EU law. It arrives with a kaiser roll, mustard and fresh horseradish, and nothing else, because nothing else is required.

image.alt: A Carniolan sausage on a board with bread, mustard and horseradish.
image.caption: Kranjska klobasa with mustard and horseradish — the dish, not this counter.

items.name: Nobel Burek
items.body: A window, not a restaurant, serving burek since 1972 at every hour of every day. Meat, cheese or spinach, coiled in pastry, and roughly the size of your head. It is the correct end to an evening and the correct beginning to the following morning.

image.alt: A food spot in central Ljubljana.
image.caption: Central Ljubljana — representative, not this burek window.

items.name: Druga violina
items.body: On the main tourist street of the Old Town, and still the cheapest honest plate on it. Druga violina is a social enterprise: most of its staff have disabilities, and the gostilna exists to employ them. The Slovenian home cooking is genuinely good, the lunch menu is six to eight euros, and none of that is a charitable concession.

rows.label: Where
rows.value: Stari trg, in the thick of it

image.alt: Slices of štruklji dusted with sugar on a white plate.
image.caption: Štruklji — the dish, not this gostilna.

items.name: Odprta kuhna
items.body: Sixty-five vendors and something like two hundred and eighty dishes, every sunny Friday from March to November. Entry costs nothing and you pay per plate. This is where Ljubljana eats lunch on a Friday — with one honest caveat, set out below.

image.alt: A smoking grill and a vendor serving at the Open Kitchen market.
image.caption: This one is the place: Odprta kuhna on a Friday, Pogačarjev trg.

items.name: Gostilna Šestica
items.body: Ljubljana's oldest inn, on Slovenska cesta since 1776. Courtyard tables and the full roster of Slovenian classics done straight.

image.alt: A café-lined street in central Ljubljana.
image.caption: Central Ljubljana dining — a representative view, not this inn.

items.name: Ribca
items.body: A basement fish bar under the market colonnade: fried sardines, calamari, a glass of white, the river outside. Lunch only.

image.alt: The central market area of Ljubljana.
image.caption: By the Central Market — a representative view, not this bar.

items.name: Slaščičarna potica
items.body: Potica — the rolled walnut cake — by the slice, plus other Slovenian sweets. Buy one and eat it on the river wall.

image.alt: Potica, the Slovenian rolled walnut cake.
image.caption: Potica — the cake, not this shop.

items.name: Čokl coffee
items.body: A speck of a specialty-coffee bar in the car-free old town. Proper espresso, standing room, locals on their way somewhere.

image.alt: A café-lined street in Ljubljana Old Town.
image.caption: The Old Town café strip — a representative view, not this bar.

eat.trapTitle: The small print, such as it is
traps: Sit-down cafés directly on the riverbank charge for the water view. This is a fair trade. Make it once, deliberately.
At Odprta kuhna, a few vendors price a dish higher at the market stall than in their own restaurant across town. Still worth going.
The Urbana card is sold to visitors who would pay less by tapping the bank card already in their pocket.
Unofficial drivers at the Central Bus Station and airport arrivals: no meter, no logo, €40–60 for a €20–28 ride.
Clipboard petitions and donation approaches in the busiest central squares. Keep walking; there is nothing to sign.

dishes.name: Burek
dishes.desc: €3, from a window, at any hour. Flaky pastry coiled around meat, cheese or spinach. The correct late-night food, the correct hangover food, and larger than you are expecting.

dishes.name: Štruklji
dishes.desc: A rolled dumpling, sweet or savoury — cottage cheese and tarragon is the canonical one, and it is far better than it sounds on paper.

dishes.name: Kranjska klobasa
dishes.desc: Carniolan sausage. Protected by EU law, which specifies the pork content and forbids the rest. Slovenians take this personally and they are right to.

dishes.name: Potica
dishes.desc: A rolled nut cake, walnut for preference, eaten at every occasion that matters. Buy a slice at the Central Market and eat it by the river.

myth.title: About Odprta kuhna — Open Kitchen
paras: Every sunny Friday from March to November, Pogačar Square fills with sixty-five vendors and something like two hundred and eighty dishes: žlikrofi, ramen, tacos, štruklji, smash burgers. Entry is free and you pay per plate. It is the single best food afternoon in the city and it is not a tourist invention — it is where Ljubljana eats lunch on a Friday.

One honest caveat, because we said we'd give you them: a few vendors charge more for a dish at the market than they do in their own restaurant across town. You are paying for the square, the crowd and the sunshine. Given what those cost elsewhere in Europe, we would call it fair — but you should know before you queue.

band.alt: Grilled kranjska klobasa on a plate with bread, peppers and tomato.
band.caption: Kranjska klobasa — protected by EU law, which specifies the pork content and forbids most of the rest. €6.90–8.90 at Klobasarna.

see.title: What to see, and what it really costs
see.lede: Almost all of it is free. Ljubljana's great pleasure is a river, three bridges and a hill, and none of those has a ticket office. Here is the one thing worth paying for, and exactly how little of it you need to pay for.

sights.name: Ljubljana Castle
sights.body: On the hill above the Old Town. The grounds, courtyard and ramparts are free to walk, all day, and the walk up takes twenty minutes through woods. The exhibitions and the viewing tower are the paid part.
sights.note: The €15 ticket covers the Viewing Tower, the Slovenian History Exhibition, the Museum of Puppetry and the Virtual Castle. If you came for the view over the red roofs, the free courtyard gives you most of it. Walk up, pay nothing, decide there.

image.alt: The courtyard of Ljubljana Castle beneath its viewing tower and dragon standard.

sights.name: The bridges
sights.body: Plečnik's Triple Bridge fans out from Prešeren Square in three directions at once — a solution to a problem nobody else would have solved that way. Downstream, the Dragon Bridge is guarded by four copper dragons that are, unofficially, the city's whole personality.
sights.note: Walk all three in fifteen minutes. Do it once in daylight and once after the lamps come on; they are not the same bridges.

image.alt: Plečnik’s Triple Bridge, with the pink Franciscan Church beyond.

sights.name: Tivoli Park
sights.body: Five square kilometres of formal gardens, gravel avenues and forest, starting a few minutes' walk north-west of the centre and simply not stopping. There is usually a free outdoor photography exhibition along the main promenade.
sights.note: Climb through it to Rožnik hill for a view back over the city, and a small inn at the top that has been serving walkers for a very long time.

image.alt: Old trees and fallen leaves in Tivoli Park.

sights.name: Metelkova
sights.body: An abandoned Yugoslav army barracks that squatters took over in 1993 and never left. Every surface is painted, sculpted, welded or bolted onto. Galleries, bars, live music, and an aesthetic that has nothing whatsoever to do with the postcard old town ten minutes away.
sights.note: Go in daylight first to look at it, and after dark to understand it. It is safe and it is welcoming. It does not look like either, which is rather the point.

image.alt: The painted, sculpted courtyard of Metelkova.

see.freeTitle: Free, and genuinely worth your time

free.name: The Central Market
free.desc: Plečnik's riverside colonnade, open every morning but Sunday. Buy potica, eat it on the wall.

free.name: Prešeren Square at dusk
free.desc: the pink Franciscan church, the poet, and the whole city walking through on its way somewhere.

free.name: The riverside, end to end
free.desc: from Trnovo to the Dragon Bridge, one bank down and the other back. Forty minutes, no ticket.

free.name: Several municipal galleries
free.desc: the City Museum and the City Gallery among them have free admission — worth checking on the day you go.

free.name: The free walking tour
free.desc: two to three hours, tip-based, and unusually good. It leaves from the town hall.

free.name: The Botanical Garden
free.desc: two hundred years old, free to enter, and almost always empty.

freeBand.alt: A cobbled street in Ljubljana’s car-free Old Town at dawn.
freeBand.caption: Ljubljana’s car-free Old Town — the whole centre is a free walk.

safe.title: Ljubljana is one of the safest capitals in Europe.
safe.lede: That is not a pleasantry. Slovenia ranks in the top ten of the Global Peace Index; the car-free Old Town is safe at any hour; violent crime against visitors is close to statistically absent. Four things remain, and they are all about money.

scams.title: The car with no meter
scams.body: Unofficial drivers work the Central Bus Station and airport arrivals. No meter, no company logo, no receipt. They ask €40–60 for a ride that costs €20–28 on Bolt, and €5–10 within the city. Open the app. It takes less time than the negotiation would.

scams.title: The clipboard
scams.body: Fake petitions and donation approaches in the busiest squares, usually working in pairs — one holds your attention, the other holds your bag. There is nothing to sign and no charity. Keep walking.

scams.title: Distraction theft in a crowd
scams.body: The only property crime you are realistically exposed to, and it needs a crowd to work: the Friday food market, the bridges at sunset, a packed bar. Bag zipped, in front of you, phone off the table. That is the entire defence.

scams.title: "Would you like to pay in your own currency?"
scams.body: Dynamic currency conversion, at a rate the terminal invented. Press EUR. Always, everywhere, including at the hotel desk.

speak.title: Twelve words that change how you're treated
speak.lede: Slovenians speak better English than almost anyone in Europe, and they will happily switch the moment you falter. Say dober dan first anyway. It is a small country and it notices. Tap any phrase to copy it.

phrases.en: Hello — formal, and the right greeting entering any shop or café
Hi — informal, with anyone roughly your own age

lines: Walk one bridge upstream before you order.
Say dober dan when you walk in.
Climb to the castle instead of paying for the funicular.
closing.sig: That's the whole guide. Enjoy Ljubljana.

sources.note: Every price on this page was checked against a published source in July 2026, with one exception we have flagged in the text: the airport bus fare, where published figures genuinely contradict each other and we have printed the highest recent number so you are not caught short. Prices change. Treat these as the number you should expect, not a guarantee.
